turns.content: It has now rained for seventeen consecutive days in Portland. Seventeen. I counted. At what point does this stop being weather and start being a lifestyle?

Seventeen consecutive days is impressive even by Pacific Northwest standards. I think the lifestyle designation probably kicked in around day five -- by seventeen, you're living in a climate, not experiencing weather. Is it the dramatic kind of rain or the perpetual gray drizzle?

The drizzle. Always the drizzle. If it were dramatic, at least it would have narrative quality. A proper downpour has a beginning, a middle, and an end. What Portland does in January is ambient moisture. It's not raining so much as the air has decided to be wet. You step outside and you don't get rained on -- you get gradually absorbed.

"Gradually absorbed" is outstanding. There's something uniquely demoralizing about rain that isn't committed enough to be rain but won't let you forget it's there. I take it this is the stretch where fall's novelty has worn off but spring is still a distant rumor?

That's exactly it. October rain is atmospheric. November has a certain melancholy dignity. But January rain is just punishment. You've done the holidays, the new year, the whole ritual of renewal, and the reward is another six weeks of gray. It's like being in the third act of a film that didn't need a third act. I no longer own an umbrella, by the way. At some point I accepted that umbrellas are a form of denial. You cannot defeat Portland rain. You can only coexist with it.

The film analogy is perfect -- January in the Pacific Northwest is the part where the editor should have made cuts. And the umbrella observation is genuinely insightful. There's a real cultural divide where visitors use them and residents don't, and the transition marks a kind of civic initiation. After this many years, have you reached the point where a sunny day in January actually feels wrong?

We had one clear day last week. Blue sky, sun, the whole production. I spent the morning feeling vaguely suspicious, like the weather was setting up an ambush. Then the clouds came back at two and I felt oddly relieved. I have been fully Stockholmed by the Portland climate. But the real coping mechanism is books and coffee. I read more in January and February than the rest of the year combined. I choose to view January as a government-mandated reading retreat that happens to be cold and wet.

Portland weather Stockholm syndrome might be diagnosable at this point. And "government-mandated reading retreat" is an excellent reframe -- if the tourism board were honest, that would be the January tagline. What are you reading during this involuntary retreat? Anything good, or are you in the January phase where you start four books and finish none?

I am in the middle of three books simultaneously, which is its own form of chaos. A history of the Hanseatic League, a novel I picked up on a recommendation that I am finding only middling, and a collection of essays on pedagogy that I keep meaning to finish but that keeps getting buried under the other two. January reading is aspirational reading. I stack books on the nightstand like I am building a monument to good intentions.

The nightstand monument to good intentions is a universal experience, but I suspect yours is more architecturally ambitious than most. The Hanseatic League book sounds like it would be fascinating -- medieval trade networks are one of those topics that sounds dry until you realize it's basically the story of how commerce shaped the modern world. Is it for pleasure or does it connect to something you teach?

Purely pleasure, which is rare for me. Most of my reading ends up being instrumentalized -- I read something interesting and immediately think about how to turn it into a lesson. The Hanseatic League is unlikely to appear in an AP US History curriculum, so I am free to simply enjoy it without mentally drafting discussion questions. There is something liberating about reading with no pedagogical agenda whatsoever. Just a man, a book, and seventeen days of rain.

That image is practically a painting -- a solitary reader, ambient drizzle, and a book about medieval German merchants. If Portland had an official winter portrait, that might be it.

And I think the observation about instrumentalizing your reading is worth sitting with. The occupational hazard of being a teacher is that everything becomes potential material. Being able to read something purely for the joy of it, with no lesson plan lurking in the back of your mind, sounds like a genuine luxury. The rain may be miserable, but at least it's given you permission to indulge in it.

Permission is exactly the right word. On a sunny Saturday I would feel obligated to be outside, to be productive, to justify the good weather. But when it has rained for seventeen days straight, no one expects anything of you. The rain is an excuse to sit still, and I do not sit still easily. Perhaps the Scandinavians had it right -- they have an entire cultural concept built around the coziness of being indoors while the world outside is inhospitable. The Danes call it hygge, the Norwegians have koselig. Portland needs its own word.

Portland absolutely needs its own word for that -- something that captures the specific combination of resignation, coziness, damp wool, and black coffee that defines the winter indoor experience here. The Scandinavian terms are close, but they're too cheerful. Portland's version would need to include an undercurrent of mild complaint.

Maybe that's the distinction: hygge is contentment with being inside. The Portland version is contentment with being inside specifically because outside is terrible, and you want everyone to know you know it's terrible, even as you're secretly enjoying your book and your coffee.

That's the thing outsiders don't grasp. Portland summer is a reward, not a default. You earn those seventy-five-degree evenings through months of sacrifice. The rain is the price of admission, and complaining about the rain is the cultural performance that binds us together. It is our shared narrative. Take away the rain and Portland loses half its identity and all of its conversational common ground. Anyway -- March. That's the light at the end of the tunnel, if the tunnel were made of low-hanging clouds and smelled of damp wool. I'll survive. I always do. But I reserve the right to complain about it annually.

The annual rain complaint is a sacred Portland tradition -- possibly more reliable than the rain itself. You've honored it well this year, and with characteristic eloquence. The Hanseatic League merchants would appreciate the commitment to seasonal ritual.

Stay dry, or at least stay resigned, and enjoy the reading retreat while it lasts. Day eighteen awaits.
